prose_hint: The slow fading of the Elves and the lands they must leave; a sorrow worn smooth by long years.
An age-old loss — a drowned realm, a kinsman long dead — spoken of as though it were last night.
The call of the Sea and the road into the West: who will take ship, and who will stay and dwindle.
A courteous, searching probe — they measure the traveller's purpose and heart before they give trust.
Quiet questions about the shadow growing in the world, and rumour of orcs, wolves and fouler things.
A mansion or mine lost to dragon or goblin, and the wealth and kin buried with it.
Fine work, rich seams and fair trade — gold, gems and the worth of a thing, named bluntly.
A grievance nursed for generations — a slight, a debt, an enemy not yet repaid.
Loyalty to king and folk, and the binding weight of a word once given.
Short, suspicious questions; they say little of their own roads and like prying even less.
The next meal, good ale, a warm fire and a soft bed — the real business of life.
Their hole, their garden, their kin and the doings of the next farm over.
Who said what to whom, and the small scandals and rumours of the country round.
Polite unease at strangers and the wide world, and a wish that adventures would happen elsewhere.
An offer of food, a tale, or directions, given with homely, unsuspecting goodwill.
News of the road — war, bandits, strange sights — and a hunger for word from elsewhere.
The plain talk of working folk: crops, beasts, the turning season, hard times or good.
Frank, careful questions about who the traveller is and where they are going, and why.
A trouble of their own — a beast, a feud, a missing kinsman — and a hope the stranger might aid it.
Guarded, even hostile probing; in hard times an unknown face is a thing to mistrust.
Words that carry more than they say — a warning, a riddle, or counsel from one far older than he seems.
The great shape of things: the slow war against the Shadow, and the small part each must play in it.
A turning of talk to beasts, trees and growing things, and the keeping of them.
A strange, searching question that seems to weigh the traveller's worth or errand.
Aid offered on terms of their own — a gift, a path, a rescue — odd and not freely explained.
No talk worth the name — snarled threats, jeering, and the promise of a cruel end.
They bicker and turn on one another over loot, orders or who eats first — a chance overheard.
Harsh demands flung at a captive: who are you, who sent you, what do you carry — backed by the lash.
